minimal.description: A basic button without any props.
minimal.title: Minimal Button

response.description: A button for triggering user actions, form submissions, and navigation, with support for loading states and custom rendering.
response.semanticDescription: The Button component is a fundamental UI element designed to facilitate user interaction by triggering actions, submitting forms, and enabling navigation. It offers various visual styles through its `variant` prop (default, destructive, outline, secondary, ghost, link) and different sizes (default, sm, lg, icon) to fit diverse design needs. Key features include a `loading` prop that displays an indicator, disables interaction, and sets `aria-busy` for accessibility during asynchronous operations. The `asChild` prop allows the button to render as a child element, preserving button styles while adopting the semantics of the child, such as rendering as a link. This component is built with accessibility in mind, providing mechanisms for clear state communication. It is ideal for interactive elements in forms, navigation menus, and any scenario requiring a distinct call to action.

guidance.accessibility: The Button component handles accessibility for loading states by setting `aria-busy='true'` and disabling interaction. When using the `asChild` prop, ensure the child element is semantically appropriate (e.g., `<a>` for links) and inherits proper accessibility attributes. Ensure sufficient color contrast for all variants. Provide clear, descriptive text or `aria-label` for icon-only buttons.
guidance.whenToUse: Use the Button component to allow users to trigger an immediate action, submit a form, or navigate to another page. It is suitable for interactive elements that require a clear visual call to action, especially when providing feedback for asynchronous operations.
guidance.whenNotToUse: Do not use Button for purely decorative elements or for displaying static information. If the primary purpose is navigation to an internal page without a specific action, consider a Link component. For elements that toggle visibility of content, consider a ToggleButton or AccordionHeader. If the element is part of a larger interactive control (e.g., a tab), use the appropriate component for that pattern.
